serviceName: Get help finding a child arrangement option
printThisPageNoJs: To print this page, press Ctrl+P on Windows or Cmd+P on Mac.
printableVersion: View a printable version of the whole guide
exitThisPage: To exit this service quickly, press escape.
phaseBannerText: This is a new service. Help us improve it and give your feedback (opens in new tab).

domesticAbuse.title: Have you experienced abuse from your ex-partner?
domesticAbuse.heading: Have you experienced abuse from your ex-partner?
domesticAbuse.intro: There may have been domestic abuse in the relationship if you have experienced any of the following:

bullets.violence: Abuse or violence, including physical, emotional and sexual
bullets.coercive: Coercive control and psychological abuse (threats, humiliation, intimidation, degradation, isolation and control)

domesticAbuse.answerYes: Answer 'Yes' if you have experienced any of the above, whether or not you have reported your experience to the police.
domesticAbuse.error: Select whether you or your children have experienced abuse from your ex-partner

childSafety.title: Are the children safe?
childSafety.heading: Are the children safe?
childSafety.intro: Children may be at risk if there has been:

bullets.domesticAbuse: any form of domestic abuse or violence, even if the abuse was not directed at the children
bullets.substances: misuse of drugs, alcohol or other substances
bullets.otherConcerns: any other safety or welfare concerns that place anyone at significant risk of harm

childSafety.error: Select whether the children are safe

childSafetyHelp.title: Getting help if your children are not safe

content.description: It is important to remember that there is help and support available to you if you or your children are unsafe.
content.warningText: Contact the police if a child you're responsible for is in immediate danger or at risk of being taken out of the UK without your consent.

wantToContinue.title: If you want to continue using this service
wantToContinue.description: If you feel confident that you can carry on without any danger to yourself or the children, you can continue using the service.

immediateRisk.title: If there is an immediate risk to your children
immediateRisk.content: If there is a risk of harm to a child, a risk that they may be unlawfully taken from the UK, or that a child that is currently outside of England and Wales may be unlawfully held, you can apply for an urgent court hearing about child arrangements.
immediateRisk.alsoFind: You can also find out how to get your child back from abroad or arrange contact.

domesticAbuse.title: If you or your children have experienced domestic abuse
domesticAbuse.content: If you have experienced any kind of domestic abuse, you can apply for a court order to protect yourself and your child.

helpAndSupport.title: Help and support
helpAndSupport.description: There is free, confidential support available to help you and your family.

rowOne.heading: Refuge's National Domestic Abuse Helpline
rowOne.data:
Free, confidential advice, 24 hours a day.

Telephone: 0808 2000 247

rowTwo.heading: Live Fear Free (Wales)
rowTwo.data:
24 hour service with specialists who make referrals and link to other services and advice.

Telephone: 0808 80 10 100

rowThree.heading: Reunite International Child Abduction Centre
rowThree.data:
An independent charity that provides advice and support on the issues surrounding the international movement of children.

Telephone: 0116 2556 234

start.title: Get help finding a child arrangement option

whoShouldUse.title: Who should use this service
whoShouldUse.description: Use this service to find out about your options for making child arrangements in England and Wales if you are separating from your child's other parent.
whoShouldUse.avoidCourt: You may be able to avoid going to court if you can agree on things like:

bullets.timeSpent: how much time they'll spend with each person

whoShouldUse.childArrangements: This is called making child arrangements (sometimes known as 'child custody' or 'child contact').

whoShouldNotUse.title: Who should not use this service
whoShouldNotUse.parentalResponsibility: You should not use this service if the court has removed or restricted parental responsibility for anyone in relation to a child.
whoShouldNotUse.financialArrangements: This service is not for making financial arrangements. Find out more about child maintenance for information on how a child's living costs can be paid when their parents separate.

content.circumstances: You will be asked questions about your circumstances, including your current relationship with your ex-partner.
content.personalDetails: You will not be asked for any personal details and your answers will not be saved or shared with anyone.
content.tenMinutes: It takes about 10 minutes to use this service.

exitTheService.title: If you need to exit the service quickly
exitTheService.description: Each page of this service has an 'Exit this page' button to help keep you safe and protect your privacy if you need it.

content.makingChildArrangements: Making child arrangements if you divorce or separate

content.howToStart: If you are unsure how you can start making child arrangements with your ex-partner, you can get help.

ifYouNeedHelp.title: If you need help deciding how to make child arrangements
ifYouNeedHelp.content: Use the 'Get help finding a child arrangement option' service to find out about your options for making child arrangements if you are a parent separating from your child's other parent.

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.content:
You will be asked questions about your circumstances, including your current relationship with your ex-partner. You will not be asked for any personal details.

It takes about 10 minutes to use this service.

exitTheService.title: If you need to exit the service quickly
exitTheService.content: Each page of this service has an 'Exit this page' button to help keep you safe and protect your privacy if you need it.

getHelpChildArrangementOption.title: Get help finding a child arrangement option
getHelpChildArrangementOption.content: Your answers will not be saved or shared with anyone.

supportWhenMakingChildArrangements.title: If you need support when making child arrangements
supportWhenMakingChildArrangements.content:
Search for services to help you make child arrangements, including legal aid and impartial advice services.

Find child arrangement support services

contactChildArrangements.title: Are you comfortable contacting your ex-partner about child arrangements?

options.second: No, I am not comfortable contacting them
options.third: I do not have their contact details
options.fourth: I can contact them but they do not respond

contactChildArrangements.error: Select whether you are comfortable contacting your ex-partner

agreement.title: Do you and your ex-partner agree on child arrangements?

descriptionList.second: when they spend time with each parent
descriptionList.third: handovers, holidays and whatever else matters to your children

options.first: Yes, we agree on some or most things
options.third: We have not discussed it yet

agreement.error: Select whether you and your ex-partner agree on child arrangements

helpToAgree.title: What would help you both agree on child arrangements?

options.first: A plan we can follow ourselves
options.second: Someone else to guide our conversations
options.third: We cannot agree – someone else needs to make a decision for us

helpToAgree.error: Select what would help you and your ex-partner to agree on child arrangements

otherOptions.title: Have you tried any other options?

content.description: Have you tried any other ways of making child arrangements in the last 4 months?
content.mediation: If you decide to go to court, you'll usually need to show you've tried mediation or another way of agreeing first.
content.court: If you have already tried mediation, arbitration or negotiating using a solicitor, the court will consider this when you apply.

options.yes: Yes, we have tried mediation or a similar method
options.no: No, we have not tried yet

otherOptions.error: Select whether you have tried any other ways of making child arrangements in the last 4 months

courtOrder.title: Explore: Applying for a court order

whyRightForYou.title: Why this could be right for you
whyRightForYou.canApplyIf: You can apply for a court order if:

reasons.cannotAgree: you cannot agree after mediation or getting help another way
reasons.domesticAbuse: there's been domestic abuse or you or the children are at risk
reasons.consentOrder: you're applying for a consent order to make your parenting plan legally binding

thingsToConsider.title: Important things to consider
thingsToConsider.mediation: Mediation Information and Assessment Meeting (MIAM): You will usually need to show you have attended this short meeting with a mediator or tried another way to agree outside of court in the 4 months before you apply for a court order. If you cannot contact your ex-partner, you still need to attend the initial MIAM alone before applying to go to court, unless you are exempt.

miamExemption.warning: You do not have to go to a MIAM if you can show there is a risk:

reasons.life: to your life, freedom, physical safety or home
reasons.unlawfullyTaken: that a child would be unlawfully taken from the UK, or a risk that a child who is currently outside England and Wales will be unlawfully held

miamExemption.moreInfo: If any of these apply, you can apply for an urgent court hearing about child arrangements

thingsToConsider.cost: Cost: It costs £263 to apply for a court order. You may be able to get legal aid to help pay court fees if you're on benefits or a low income.
thingsToConsider.time: Time: It usually takes around 10 months to get a court order, depending on where you live and your situation.

courtOrder.title: Court orders you can apply for

descriptionList.first: Child arrangements order: To decide where your child lives and the time they spend with each parent
descriptionList.second: Specific issue order: If you need the court to make a decision on a question like what school they go to
descriptionList.third: Prohibited steps order: To stop the other parent from making a decision about the child's upbringing

otherWays.title: Other ways to agree without going to court
otherWays.description: Court is the right option for some families. If you want to try another way to agree without going to court, you can look into:

mediation.title: Mediation
mediation.description: This could suit you if: You'd like a professional to help you and your ex-partner agree on the details of a child arrangement. You do not have to be in the same room or video call as your ex-partner.
mediation.inset: You can usually get a voucher worth up to £500 to pay for family mediation through the Family Mediation Voucher Scheme, regardless of what you earn.
mediation.alreadyTried: Already tried mediation? You can always try again, for example if your situation or child's needs have changed.

otherOptions.title: Other options to explore
otherOptions.description: There are other ways to help you agree on child arrangements outside of court. These are sometimes known as 'non-court dispute resolution' (NCDR) and are usually quicker than going to court. The type you choose will depend on your circumstances, for example your financial situation, where you live and whether you're in contact with your ex-partner.
otherOptions.link: Find out more about other ways to agree without going to court

helpAndSupport.title: Help and support
helpAndSupport.description: These are some of the services you may find helpful for applying for a court order.

adviceNow.name: Advicenow
adviceNow.description: Information on what to do before applying for a court order from a charity that provides easy-to-understand information for people facing everyday legal issues with limited access to a lawyer.

cafcass.name: Children and Family Court Advisory and Support Service (Cafcass)
cafcass.description: Advice on the court process from Cafcass, which looks after the interests of children in family court cases in England.

lawWorks.name: LawWorks
lawWorks.description: A charity which connects volunteer lawyers with people needing legal advice, who aren't eligible for legal aid and can't afford to pay a lawyer.

naccc.name: National Association of Child Contact Centres (NACCC)
naccc.description: Information on services available for children to maintain or re-establish contact with a parent from a charity dedicated to providing safe spaces where children can meet the parents they don't live with.

makingArrangements.text: Making child arrangements if you divorce or separate

rightForYou.title: Why this could be right for you

content.chooseAProfessional: Mediation is when you and your ex-partner choose a professional to help you agree on the details of child arrangements.
content.sameRoom: You do not have to be in the same room or call as your ex-partner.

content.info: If you or your children have experienced abuse from your ex-partner, tell the mediator. They can advise on the safest way to continue, or whether you should apply for a court order.
content.initialMeeting: Initial meeting: You may need to go to a short meeting with a mediator to assess if mediation is the right option for you. This is sometimes called a Mediation Information and Assessment Meeting (MIAM).
content.cost: Cost: Mediation is usually cheaper and quicker than going to court. You may be able to get legal aid to cover the cost if you are on a low income. The usual cost of a MIAM is around £120.
content.costInset: You can usually get a voucher worth up to £500 to pay for family mediation through the Family Mediation Voucher Scheme, regardless of what you earn.
content.stayInControl: You stay in control: Unlike in court, where a judge makes the decisions about child arrangements, in mediation you and your ex-partner decide what you agree to and what you don't.
content.childInclusiveMediation: Child-inclusive mediation: Children can have a voice in the process if it's suitable. A mediator can talk to your child about what they want to make sure their views are heard.
content.courtOption: Court is still an option: If the mediator does not think mediation is right for you, they might advise you to go to court instead.

otherWaysToAgree.title: Other ways to agree without going to court
otherWaysToAgree.description: If you want to try another way to agree without going to court, you can look into:

makingAParentingPlan.title: Making a parenting plan
makingAParentingPlan.description: This could suit you if: If you and your ex-partner can agree on child arrangements, you can record it in a child arrangements plan, also called a 'parenting plan'.
makingAParentingPlan.link: Explore: Making a parenting plan

otherOptionsToExplore.title: Other options to explore
otherOptionsToExplore.description: There are other ways to help you agree on child arrangements outside of court. These are sometimes known as 'non-court dispute resolution' (NCDR) and are usually quicker than going to court. The type you choose will depend on your circumstances, for example your financial situation, where you live and whether you're in contact with your ex-partner.
otherOptionsToExplore.link: Find out more about other ways to agree without going to court

helpAndSupport.title: Help and support
helpAndSupport.description: These are some of the services you may find helpful for making child arrangements by mediation.
helpAndSupport.mediationCouncilText: Find a trained mediator from a network of family mediation organisations in England and Wales.
helpAndSupport.legalAidAdviserLink: Find a legal aid adviser or family mediator
helpAndSupport.legalAidAdviserText: Use this service to find legal aid advisers in England and Wales.
helpAndSupport.adviceNowText: Information on how family mediation can help you from a charity that provides easy-to-understand information for people facing everyday legal issues with limited access to a lawyer.

content.makingChildArrangements: Making child arrangements if you divorce or separate

whyRightForYou.title: Why this could be right for you
whyRightForYou.noPaperwork: You do not have to go to court or do any official paperwork if you and your ex-partner agree about child arrangements.
whyRightForYou.informalArrangements: Informal arrangements where nothing is written down work for some people, but if you'd prefer, you can record what you have agreed in a child arrangements plan, also called a 'parenting plan'.

content.warningText: It may not be appropriate for you to make child arrangements directly with your ex-partner if there is any risk of harm to you or the children. Find out how to get help with domestic abuse.

thingsToConsider.title: Important things to consider
thingsToConsider.noCourt: No court: You do not have to go to court to make informal arrangements or a parenting plan.
thingsToConsider.noCost: No cost: You can make a parenting plan using free online tools and templates.
thingsToConsider.easyToChange: Easy to review and change: As your children get older and their needs change, or if your situation changes.
thingsToConsider.legallyBinding: Make it legally binding: You can get a legal adviser and apply to the family court for a 'consent order' based on your plan if you want to make an informal agreement legally binding. You will need to apply for a court order to get your consent order approved, but there's usually no court hearing.
thingsToConsider.findOutMore: Find out more about what to do if you agree on child arrangements

nextStep.title: Next step: Make a plan
nextStep.description: The 'Propose a child arrangements plan' tool will help you build a plan that you can send to your ex-partner, covering:

items.spendTime: when they spend time with each parent
items.handovers: handovers, holidays and whatever else matters to your children

otherWays.title: Other ways to agree without going to court
otherWays.description: If you want to try another way to agree without going to court, you can look into:

mediation.title: Mediation
mediation.description: This could suit you if: You'd like a professional to help you and your ex-partner agree on the details of a child arrangement. You do not have to be in the same room or call as your ex-partner.
mediation.inset: You can usually get a voucher worth up to £500 to pay for family mediation through the Family Mediation Voucher Scheme, regardless of what you earn.

helpAndSupport.title: Help and support
helpAndSupport.description: These are some of the services you may find helpful for making child arrangements with your ex-partner.

adviceNow.name: Advicenow
adviceNow.description: Information on child arrangements from a charity that provides online legal information and support tools for people facing everyday legal issues with limited access to a lawyer.

cafcass.name: Children and Family Court Advisory and Support Service (Cafcass)
cafcass.description: Use the 'Our Child's Plan' tool from Cafcass to make a parenting plan. Cafcass looks after the interests of children in family court cases in England.

cafcassCymru.name: Cafcass Cymru
cafcassCymru.description: A guide to making a parenting plan from the organisation that provides expert child-focused advice and support in family courts across Wales.

makingArrangements.text: Making child arrangements if you divorce or separate

safeguarding.title: Getting help if you have experienced abuse

content.description: You may have been in an abusive relationship. Domestic abuse or violence is a crime. Domestic abuse does not only mean physical violence – it includes emotional, sexual, psychological and financial abuse, as well as harassment and stalking.
content.descriptionSupport: It is important to remember that there is help and support available to you.
content.warningText: If you are in immediate danger, call 999 and ask for the police.

wantToContinue.title: If you want to continue using this service
wantToContinue.description: If you feel confident that you can carry on without any danger to yourself or the children, you can continue using the service.

getHelpProtecting.title: Get help protecting yourself and your children

content.domesticAbuse: If you have experienced any kind of domestic abuse, you can apply for a court order to protect yourself and your child.
content.citizensAdvice: Citizens Advice has advice on making child arrangements when there's been domestic abuse.
content.findOut: Find out how to get help with domestic abuse.
content.specificHelp: If you want to access help specifically to cater to your background and needs or want support and help for specific types of abuse there are several organisations that can help - see Domestic abuse: specialist sources of support.

helpAndSupport.title: Help and support
helpAndSupport.description: There is free, confidential support available to help you and your family.

rowOne.heading: Refuge's National Domestic Abuse Helpline
rowOne.data:
Free, confidential advice, 24 hours a day.

Telephone: 0808 2000 247

rowTwo.heading: Live Fear Free (Wales)
rowTwo.data:
24 hour service with specialists who make referrals and link to other services and advice.

Telephone: 0808 80 10 100

rowThree.heading: Men's Advice Line
rowThree.data:
A supportive helpline for all male victims of domestic abuse and those supporting them.

Telephone: 0808 801 0327

optionsNoContact.title: Options to explore if you are not comfortable contacting your ex-partner

content.notComfortable: If you are not comfortable contacting your ex-partner, there are still ways you can make child arrangements without going to court.
content.solicitorCanContact: With these options, your mediator or solicitor can contact your ex-partner for you.

content.thisCouldSuitYou: This could suit you if: You do not want to have direct contact with your ex-partner.
content.attendSession: Shuttle mediation is where you and your ex-partner both attend a session with a mediator, but sit in different rooms or online meetings so you do not have to have any contact.
content.mediatorMoves: The mediator moves between rooms, listening to you both and helping you come to an agreement about child arrangements.
content.getVoucher: You can usually get a voucher worth up to £500 to pay for family mediation through the Family Mediation Voucher Scheme, regardless of what you earn.
content.nextStep: Next step: Find out more about mediation

content.thisCouldSuitYou: This could suit you if: You cannot contact your ex-partner or they do not reply to you.
content.legalAdviser: A family solicitor can help you negotiate with your ex-partner without going to court. This is known as 'solicitor negotiation' or 'lawyer negotiation'.
content.speakToExPartner: You do not have to speak to your ex-partner if you choose a solicitor to negotiate for you.
content.nextStep: Next step: Find a legal adviser from the Law Society

content.thisCouldSuitYou: This could suit you if: You want to focus on collaboration and practicality.
content.hireLegalAdviser: You and your ex-partner can each hire your own legal advisers to help you negotiate. You then all meet to try and agree on child arrangements.
content.nextStep: Next step: Find out more about collaborative law from Resolution

otherWaysToAgree.title: Other ways to agree without going to court
otherWaysToAgree.description: There are other ways to help you agree on child arrangements outside of court. These are sometimes known as 'non-court dispute resolution' (NCDR) and are usually quicker than going to court. The type you choose will depend on your circumstances, for example your financial situation, where you live and whether you're in contact with your ex-partner.
otherWaysToAgree.link: Find out more about other ways to agree without going to court

content.description: These are some of the services you may find helpful for making child arrangements.
content.resolutionText: Find a law professional - including collaborative practitioners - committed to a non-confrontational approach to help you resolve your family issue.
content.adviceNowText: Information on child arrangements from a charity that provides online legal information and support tools for people facing everyday legal issues with limited access to a lawyer.

content.makingChildArrangements: Making child arrangements if you divorce or separate

content.putSmallFiles: The Get help finding a child arrangement service puts small files (known as 'cookies') onto your computer to collect information about how you browse the service.
content.measure: measure how you use the service so it can be updated and improved based on your needs
content.identifyYouPersonally: Get help finding a child arrangement cookies aren't used to identify you personally.
content.messageBeforeStore: You'll normally see a message before we store a cookie on your computer.
content.manageCookiesLink: Find out more about how to manage cookies

howCookiesAreUsed.title: How cookies are used in this service

essentialCookies.title: Essential cookies
essentialCookies.description: Essential cookies are needed for the service to work. We do not need to ask for permission to use them.

rememberingProgress.title: Remembering your progress
rememberingProgress.description: We will store cookies to remember your application progress in this computer and to expire your session after 120 minutes of inactivity or when you close your browser.

valueRow.purpose: Saves your current progress in this computer and tracks inactivity periods
valueRow.expires: After 120 minutes of inactivity or when you close your browser

content.seeBanner: You may see a banner when you visit Get help finding a child arrangement option inviting you to accept or reject optional cookies.
content.setCookies: We'll set cookies so that your computer knows you've seen it and not to show it again, and also to store your settings.

content.googleAnalyticsSoftware: We use Google Analytics software to collect information about how you use Get help finding a child arrangement option. We do this to help make sure the service is meeting the needs of its users and to help us make improvements.
content.howLongYouSpend: how long you spend on each page
content.howYouGotToService: how you got to the service
content.whatYouClick: what you click on while you're visiting the service
content.weDoNotCollect: We don't collect or store your personal information (for example your name or address) so this information can't be used to identify who you are.
content.setsTheCookies: Google Analytics sets the following cookies:

valueRowOne.name: _ga
valueRowOne.purpose: This helps us count how many people visit Get help finding a child arrangement option by tracking if you've visited before

valueRowTwo.name: _ga_
valueRowTwo.purpose: Used by Google Analytics to find and track an individual session with your device

acceptAnalyticsCookies.title: Do you want to accept analytics cookies?

content.reportProblem: To report a problem or suggest improvements to this service, you can email:
content.feedbackEmailAddress: This email address should only be used for feedback on the digital service.
content.cannotAnswerQuestions: We cannot answer any questions about child arrangements.

content.committed: The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) is committed to the protection and security of your personal information.
content.notice: This notice describes how and why we are using such information.
content.noticeDescription: This privacy notice describes how we collect and use personal information during and after your relationship with us, in accordance with data protection law. This notice will be updated regularly.

typesOfPersonalData.title: The type of personal data we process

content.collectAndProcessInformation: We collect and process information related to child arrangement planning to help you identify child arrangement planning options suitable for your circumstances. However, we do not collect personally identifiable information (PII), such as names, addresses, or contact details.
content.gatheringInformation: Because we are not gathering information that can be used to identify you, many data protection rights do not apply.
content.typesOfInformation: The types of information that we collect which are not personally identifiable may include:
content.userInteractions: user interactions with our platform, including navigation behaviour and feature usage, to improve our service
content.doNotShareAnswers: We do not share the answers you provide with any other organisation or government department.

dataProtectionOfficer.description: If you have any concerns about our use of your personal data, you can contact the MoJ data protection officer:
dataProtectionOfficer.address:
Data Protection Officer
Ministry of Justice
10th Floor, Post Point 10.31
102 Petty France
London
SW1H 9AJ

informationCommissionersOffice.description: You can also complain to the ICO if you are unhappy with how we have used your data.

content.statement: This statement was prepared on 22 January 2026.

responsibility.title: Responsibility for the service
responsibility.description: The Ministry of Justice (MOJ) runs this service. We try to make sure the information we provide is accurate and complete and the service is always available and virus free.
responsibility.noLiability: The MoJ accepts no liability for:

items.unableToAccess: you being unable to access the service (for example due to high demand or because we've taken it down for maintenance)
items.indirectDamages: indirect damages arising from you using the service or relying on its content
items.externalWebsites: the content and availability of external websites that link to the service or which link from it

information.title: Information about you and your site visits
information.collectAndProcess: We collect and process information related to:

items.childArrangement: child arrangement planning to help you identify child arrangement planning options suitable for your circumstances
items.howYouUse: how you use the service to help make sure it is meeting the needs of its users and to help us make improvements

information.agreeToCollect: By using this service you agree to us collecting this information.
information.doNotCollectPII: However, we do not collect personally identifiable information (PII), such as names, addresses, or contact details, in accordance with our privacy notice and cookie policy.

limitations.title: Limitations of the service
limitations.description: Using this service does not guarantee that you will be able to resolve your child arrangements.

misuse.title: Misuse
misuse.description: You must not impersonate another person, provide false or misleading information, or use this service on behalf of someone else without their consent.

restrictions.title: Restrictions on use
restrictions.description: If you have a court order in place that restricts your contact with the other parent or child, such as a no contact order, non-molestation order, or any other legal restriction, you must not use this service to contact them.
restrictions.legalConsequences: Doing so could be a breach of the order and may have legal consequences. If you are unsure whether a court order or other legal restriction affects your ability to use this service, seek legal advice before proceeding.

content.appliesTo: This accessibility statement applies to the 'Get help finding a child arrangement option' service.
content.runBy: This website is run by the Ministry of Justice (MoJ). We want as many people as possible to be able to use this website. For example, that means you should be able to:

abilities.changeColours: change colours, contrast levels and fonts using browser or device settings
abilities.zoomIn: zoom in up to 400% without the text spilling off the screen
abilities.navigate: navigate most of the website using a keyboard or speech recognition software
abilities.listen: listen to most of the website using a screen reader (including the most recent versions of JAWS, NVDA and VoiceOver)

content.simpleText: We've also made the website text as simple as possible to understand.
content.abilityNet: AbilityNet has advice on making your device easier to use if you have a disability.

howAccessible.title: How accessible this website is
howAccessible.tested: We have tested this service with assistive technologies including screen readers and voice-to-text technology.
howAccessible.notFullyTested: However, the service has not yet had a full test against the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2 AA standard, so there may be further issues we are unaware of.

feedback.title: Feedback and contact information
feedback.contact: If you find any problems not listed on this page or think we're not meeting accessibility requirements, email childarrangements@justice.gov.uk.

enforcement.title: Enforcement procedure
enforcement.responsible: The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) is responsible for enforcing the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018 (the 'accessibility regulations').
enforcement.complaint: If you're not happy with how we respond to your complaint, contact the Equality Advisory and Support Service (EASS).

technical.title: Technical information about this website's accessibility
technical.commitment: The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) is committed to making its website accessible, in accordance with the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018.

compliance.title: Compliance status
compliance.tested: The service has been tested by the MOJ Accessibility Team (Summer 2025) and user research has been carried out internally with users with access needs. Most issues identified in this testing were addressed. The service has not yet had a full test against the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2 AA standard. This testing is planned to take place in Summer 2026.
compliance.partiallyCompliant: This website is partially compliant with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines version 2.2 AA standard, due to the non-compliances listed below.

nonCompliance.title: Non-compliance with the accessibility regulations
nonCompliance.exitButton: Screen reader users may experience the following issues with the 'Exit this page' button, which allows you to leave the service quickly.

issues.lastElement: The button is read as the last element on a page, but is present on all pages in the service.
issues.shiftKey: The button can be activated by clicking the Shift key three times if navigating using a keyboard. As the Shift key is a common navigation shortcut in screen reader software, the button may accidentally be activated.

nonCompliance.planToResolve: We plan to resolve these issues by Summer 2026.

preparation.title: Preparation of this accessibility statement

preparation.lastTested: This service was last tested in Summer 2025 against the WCAG 2.2 AA standard.

cookieBanner.title: Cookies on Get help finding a child arrangement option
cookieBanner.useEssentialCookies: We use some essential cookies to make this service work.
cookieBanner.useAnalyticsCookies: We’d also like to use analytics cookies so we can understand how you use the service and make improvements.

cookieBanner.rejectedAnalyticsCookies: You have rejected analytics cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time.
